subtitle: Player-Coach · Consulting · Regulated Delivery · Biotech
hero_sub: SM and PM at the same time, embedded at the client, accountable to both the firm and the stakeholder. That's not a new mode for me — it's the only mode I've ever operated in.

the_case.need: 5+ years SM/Agile Coach — proven delivery track record
the_case.answer: 10+ years. DigitasLBI/SunTrust (2014), Underline (2015), A&E ~10 teams (2016–2018), Paperless Post (2018–2019), Understood.org (2019–2020), Stealth AI (2024), OVRSTCH (2024–2026), Crocs (2026).
the_case.detail: The SM track record is not a line item — it is the consistent pattern across the entire career. Each engagement has a different client, a different domain, a different team, and the same outcomes: ceremonies running cleanly, blockers surfacing before they become delivery misses, and teams that own their process by the time the engagement ends. The 10+ years figure is conservative — the first client-embedded delivery work was in 2006.

the_case.answer: Crocs: player-coach SM and PM for a SOX-compliant release program. Eptura: Agile Coach + PMO lead across ~10 teams. Stealth AI: SM + delivery consultant + BA. This is the consistent pattern, not a capability I'm adding.
the_case.detail: The player-coach model requires holding two simultaneous frames: at the team level (ceremonies, coaching, blockers, velocity) and at the program level (dependencies, risks, stakeholder reporting, milestone coordination). At Crocs I ran ceremony cadence and go/no-go governance simultaneously. At Eptura I facilitated team-level retrospectives and reported to senior leadership in the same week. The two frames are not in conflict — the program-level view makes the team-level coaching more accurate, and the team-level depth makes the program reporting more credible.

the_case.need: Deep, practical Jira expertise — backlog, reporting, portfolio tracking
the_case.answer: Built Jira from scratch at A&E (~10 teams, zero prior practice). Standardized across Eptura PMO (~10 teams). Daily use for backlog management, velocity, burndown, and executive dashboards at every role since 2014.
the_case.detail: Jira depth means different things at different levels. At A&E I set up the entire instance from zero — workflows, user groups, boards, and all artifacts — for ~10 teams that had been using Post-it notes. At Eptura I standardized Jira across a multi-team PMO, creating governance-aligned dashboards that senior leadership used for portfolio health. At Crocs I embedded Jira AI into daily delivery workflows, automating sprint summaries and decision documentation. The tool is not the skill — the skill is knowing what to build in Jira so that the delivery visibility it creates is actually useful to the people who need it.

the_case.need: Consulting pedigree — client-facing, boutique firm track record
the_case.answer: Every major role in this career was a client-embedded consulting engagement: Crocs (via Creative Circle), Meta (contract), Eptura (contract), SunTrust/DigitasLBI (agency on-site at a bank), A&E, Understood.org, SapientNitro, THINK Inc, 22squared. This is not a transition — it's the model.
the_case.detail: Boutique consulting is a different operating mode than in-house employment. The consultant's reputation is the firm's reputation. There is no institutional backstop if the engagement goes sideways — the SM is the engagement. I have operated in that mode for twelve years. At DigitasLBI I was one of seven consultants on-site daily at SunTrust. At SapientNitro I represented the firm to Unilever across multiple brand platforms. At Crocs I was the sole delivery point of contact between a staffing firm and a global retailer's technology organization. Client-facing under pressure is the baseline, not a differentiator.

the_case.answer: THINK Inc (2012–2013): applied Waterfall or Agile based on project scope. 22squared/Toyota: Agile methods in an agency waterfall context. Crocs: hybrid release governance (Scrum + CAB-style change approval). Multiple regulated environments requiring both.
the_case.detail: Hybrid means different things depending on who's asking. In some contexts it means using Waterfall for fixed-scope compliance deliverables while running Agile for the surrounding software work — that's Crocs. In other contexts it means using Agile methods inside a fundamentally Waterfall organization — that's most of the agency work from 2011 onward. The skill is reading which method fits which workstream and not evangelizing a framework where the constraint is actually the client's regulatory requirement.

the_case.need: Pharma/life sciences or regulated delivery domain
the_case.answer: Understood.org (2019–2020): regulated, healthcare-adjacent delivery. Privacy and confidentiality standards enforced throughout. WCAG 2.1 AA as non-negotiable Definition of Done. Distressed program recovery under genuine compliance stakes.
the_case.detail: At Understood.org I delivered a platform serving users with learning and attention differences — a regulated, healthcare-adjacent context where the compliance requirement was not optional and the wrong decision had downstream consequences. I enforced privacy and confidentiality standards throughout the delivery process and embedded WCAG 2.1 AA as a hard Definition of Done requirement. I have not delivered in a pharma regulatory context specifically. What I can tell you is that regulated delivery — where 'we'll fix it in the next sprint' is not an acceptable answer to a compliance failure — is a discipline I have practiced, not a domain I'm entering cold.

the_stack.headline: The delivery toolkit.
the_stack.subhead: The tools I use to make delivery visible and sustainable.

nodes.label: Jira & Jira AI
nodes.desc: Primary delivery tracking — built from scratch at A&E, standardized at Eptura, embedded Jira AI at Crocs for sprint automation and reporting
nodes.pm_role: Built, standardized, and coached Jira at every major engagement since 2014. Jira AI reduced administrative overhead ~30% at Crocs without sacrificing reporting quality.

nodes.label: Azure DevOps
nodes.desc: Program-level delivery tracking, dependency management, sprint boards, and leadership dashboards
nodes.pm_role: ADO configuration owner at Eptura PMO (~10 teams). Built real-time release dashboards at Crocs. Fluent in switching between Jira and ADO depending on client environment.

nodes.label: Confluence & Smartsheet
nodes.desc: Documentation, agile education materials, and program-level reporting across multi-team PMOs
nodes.pm_role: Standardized agile documentation across Eptura PMO. Built Smartsheet leadership dashboards synthesizing delivery health across ~10 teams for executive stakeholders.

nodes.label: Microsoft Copilot
nodes.desc: AI-assisted documentation, stakeholder communication drafts, and meeting synthesis
nodes.pm_role: Embedded at Crocs for delivery documentation and stakeholder communication. Frees time from administrative output to actual delivery work and coaching.

nodes.label: GitHub & CI/CD
nodes.desc: Version control and CI/CD pipeline awareness; hands-on via six production applications deployed via GitHub Actions
nodes.pm_role: Can read a PR, understand a deployment pipeline, and hold my own in a technical infrastructure conversation — not as an operator, but as a credible delivery partner to engineering teams.

when_things_break.scenario: Clinical and engineering teams are misaligned on a compliance requirement mid-sprint — engineering built to the wrong spec
response: Stop the build at risk before any more work compounds the problem
Get the specific compliance requirement in writing from the clinical SME before any engineering decision is made
Translate the compliance constraint into engineering language — what exactly needs to change, why, and what the acceptance criteria are
Facilitate a decision meeting with the right stakeholders: delay the sprint, de-scope, or accept a documented risk — no ambiguity about who made the call
Document the decision, the owner, and the rationale before work resumes — this is the audit record

when_things_break.scenario: Scope is expanding mid-sprint — stakeholders are adding requirements without going through governance
response: Name the pattern explicitly in the next standup — scope additions outside governance are a delivery risk, not a favor
Pull the sprint goal and the current task list side by side and show the delta to the team and the stakeholder
Facilitate a scope triage with Product and the requester: what is committed, what is new, who approved the change?
Add a scope change log — every new requirement gets a decision owner, a date, and a disposition
If the pattern persists, escalate to program leadership with data: here is what uncontrolled scope is doing to delivery predictability

when_things_break.scenario: A team is resisting agile coaching — 'we already do Scrum, we don't need someone telling us how to do ceremonies'
response: Validate before pushing back — sometimes the team is right and the process is actually fine
Use their own delivery data: velocity trends, sprint carryover, cycle time — let the metrics open the conversation
Find the pain point they actually have (the one they're not calling an agile problem) and connect the coaching to solving that specific thing
Run one small experiment rather than a framework adoption — one ceremony change with a measurable outcome
If the resistance is leadership-driven, escalate with data and a specific ask: here is what the metrics show, here is the intervention, here is the expected outcome

how_i_run_a_room: Every ceremony has a stated outcome — if it doesn't, it doesn't happen
Blockers surface at standup with a proposed path forward — not at retrospective as a postmortem
Sprint goals are written before planning starts — the team commits to an outcome, not a list of tasks
Scope changes go through governance — not Slack at 4pm the day before sprint end
Compliance requirements are Definition of Done items — not post-launch fixes
Status updates are written and structured for skimming — not verbal reports that require attendance
The client always knows where delivery stands — not because they asked, but because the reporting is proactive

points: SOX-compliant release governance across Engineering, QA, Change Management, and Business
Built Release Radar tracking framework — real-time visibility for leadership across environments
Jira AI + Copilot embedded in delivery: ~30% administrative overhead reduction
On-time go-live with clean post-launch stabilization under hard deadline pressure

track_record.role: Agile Coach & Senior Scrum Master — Enterprise PMO
points: ~10 distributed cross-functional teams (India + California) — Engineering, Product, UX, Data, Marketing
35%+ delivery efficiency gain; 20% sprint carryover reduction through coaching and process discipline
Spearheaded Agile adoption in Marketing department from zero to independent self-run ceremonies
Standardized Jira and built Smartsheet executive dashboards synthesizing portfolio health

track_record.role: SM & Delivery Lead — Regulated, Healthcare-Adjacent Program
points: Regulated, compliance-conscious delivery environment — privacy and WCAG 2.1 AA as non-negotiable DoD
12+ country distributed team — NY, CA, UK, Australia, Poland, Germany, France, Belgium, South America, Japan
On-time launch, no critical regressions — distressed program recovery, final crucial months
Advised VP Engineering on Agile practices, PMO structure, and SDLC methodology

track_record.role: SM & Agile Coach — Scaled Multi-Team Delivery
points: ~10 concurrent SVOD workstreams (Web, iOS, tvOS, Android, Roku) — Scrum-of-Scrums across ~6 teams
Built Jira from scratch: workflows, boards, user groups, reporting — zero prior practice on intake
Two-year cultural transformation: Post-it notes to structured, data-driven delivery
Shipped all platforms against externally committed launch windows

before: Ceremonies happen but nobody's sure what they're for — standup is a status meeting, retro is a complaint session
Scope is expanding mid-sprint because stakeholders haven't been told no through a documented process
Compliance requirements surface at QA instead of Definition of Done — the sprint is done but the build isn't shippable
Blockers reach the program manager after they've already delayed the sprint
after: Every ceremony has a stated outcome and ends with clear next owners — teams leave knowing what changed
Scope changes go through governance: documented, owned, and dispositioned before work starts
Compliance requirements are DoD items — built to spec the first time, not retrofitted under deadline pressure
Blockers surface in standup with proposed paths forward — the program manager hears about risks, not failures

the_ramp.deliverable: Meet every team member, PO, data scientist, and automation engineer. Audit current ceremony cadence, Jira setup, and delivery metrics. Understand what the client considers a successful sprint before changing anything.
Facilitate first full ceremony cycle. Identify the top 3 delivery gaps — not what the team says the problems are, but what the flow metrics show. Propose the first coaching intervention.
Jira dashboards publishing reliably for client leadership. First dependency map complete. Coaching conversations underway with POs and engineers where story quality is the bottleneck.
Client stakeholders have real-time delivery visibility without asking for it. Team is running ceremonies independently. First measurable delivery improvement is documented — not estimated.
